name: Sodium
name_origin: English 'soda'; symbol Na from Latin 'natrium' (from Arabic 'natrun', a natural mineral salt).

occurrence.abundance_summary: Sodium is the sixth most abundant element in Earth's crust (~2.27%) and the most abundant alkali metal. It is found dissolved in seawater and in many minerals such as halite, feldspar, and cryolite.

compounds.name: Sodium chloride
compounds.summary: Common table salt; essential for human nutrition and a major industrial feedstock for chlorine and sodium hydroxide.

compounds.name: Sodium hydroxide
compounds.summary: A strong base (caustic soda) used in soap making, paper production, and many chemical syntheses.

compounds.name: Sodium carbonate
compounds.summary: Soda ash, widely used in glassmaking, detergents, and water softening.

compounds.name: Sodium bicarbonate
compounds.summary: Baking soda; used as a leavening agent, antacid, and mild abrasive cleaner.

compounds.name: Sodium sulfate
compounds.summary: Used in detergents and the kraft paper-making process.

compounds.name: Sodium nitrate
compounds.summary: Chile saltpeter; historically used in fertilizers and as an oxidizer in explosives and food preservation.

uses: Production of sodium vapor lamps for street lighting
Coolant in some fast-breeder nuclear reactors due to high thermal conductivity
Reducing agent in the manufacture of metals like titanium and zirconium
Manufacture of sodium compounds (NaOH, Na2CO3) used in soaps, detergents, and glass
Heat-transfer fluid in certain industrial applications
Essential dietary electrolyte (as Na+) for nerve transmission and fluid balance
reactivity_summary: Sodium is a soft, highly reactive alkali metal that tarnishes rapidly in air and reacts vigorously with water to produce sodium hydroxide and hydrogen gas, often igniting the hydrogen. It must be stored under mineral oil or inert gas to prevent oxidation.
isotopes_summary: Sodium has only one stable isotope, Na-23. Na-22 (half-life ~2.6 years) and Na-24 (half-life ~15 hours) are the most notable radioisotopes, used in scientific research and medical tracing.

citations.source: Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Sodium
